title: Greater than or equal to Operator

content.problemStatement:
In C, the greater than or equal to (`>=`) operator is used to compare two values. It checks whether the value on the left side is greater than or equal to the value on the right side. If it is, the operator returns true (1); otherwise, it returns false (0).

**Syntax:**
```c
value1 >= value2
```

The expression `value1 >= value2` returns:
- `true` (1) if `value1` is greater than or equal to `value2`
- `false` (0) if `value1` is less than `value2`
---
**Examples:**
```c
#include

int main() {
    // Example 1: Comparing Integers
    int num1 = 25; // num1 holds an integer value of 25
    int num2 = 20; // num2 holds an integer value of 20
    printf("Example 1: %d\n", num1 >= num2);  // Outputs: 1 (true)

    // Example 2: Comparing Floating-Point Numbers
    double price1 = 15.99; // price1 holds a double value of 15.99
    double price2 = 15.99; // price2 holds a double value of 15.99
    printf("Example 2: %d\n", price1 >= price2);  // Outputs: 1 (true)

    // Example 3: Comparing Mixed Types (Int and Float)
    int x = 12;    // x holds the integer value 12
    float y = 10.5f; // y holds the float value 10.5
    printf("Example 3: %d\n", x >= y);  // Outputs: 1 (true)

    return 0;
}
```
